analysis:
Across the top scorers (Heuristics 1–6) the same effective pattern appears: Pareto prefiltering + a cheap rect‑volume (single‑point) surrogate to shortlist candidates, a lazy/stamped greedy driver that recomputes exact marginal hypervolume only when a candidate reaches the heap top, HV caching, and a bounded local single‑swap refinement. Stronger variants add (a) stochastic sampling inside the greedy loop to diversify (Heur.1), (b) diversity seeding like farthest‑first (Heur.3), and (c) careful adaptive pool/pool_size and stamp semantics to limit recomputations (Heur.2/4/6).

Weaker entries (Heuristics 7–11) lean heavily on raw Monte‑Carlo coverage or less disciplined parameter choices: they often build large sample×candidate boolean matrices, recompute many marginals eagerly, or use inconsistent MC bounding (per‑set boxes). Consequences: higher memory and CPU, noisy/non‑comparable marginal estimates, and more wasted HV evaluations. Other avoidable overheads seen across implementations: O(N^2) Python loops for nondomination, per‑call MC boxes that change sample domains (data.min vs global box), and unstructured cache keys (loss of determinism or costly hashing).

Good design tradeoffs observed:
- Shared MC samples + dominated masks give very fast marginal counts if memory allows, and combine well with lazy heaps.
- Stamped lazy heaps + hv_cache drastically reduce exact HV calls.
- Seeding by rect‑volume provides a cheap, high‑recall shortlist; diversity (farthest‑first or distance surrogates) prevents redundancy in early picks.
- Local swap passes yield consistent improvements but must be tightly bounded and focused on top outsiders.

Pitfalls to avoid:
- Per‑call varying sampling boxes (breaks comparisons).
- Large global dominated matrices without chunking or bitset compression for big M×N.
- Excessive Python loops (vectorize or use C/numba for bottlenecks).
- Overly aggressive swap budgets or huge shortlists that negate benefits of lazy evaluation.
experience: Prefilter by Pareto, build an adaptive shortlist using a blended surrogate (rect volume + normalized shared global MC dominated counts + a lightweight diversity term), then run a stamped lazy greedy heap that recomputes true gains only at the top (use pgm exact HV when available). Cache bitset dominated masks and HV results, use a tiny focused swap pool, vectorize nondomination, and adapt sample/pool sizes to N, D, k for speed and reliable marginal estimates.
current_reflection: Prefilter by Pareto, build an adaptive shortlist using a blended surrogate (rect volume + normalized shared global MC dominated counts + a lightweight diversity term), then run a stamped lazy greedy heap that recomputes true gains only at the top (use pgm exact HV when available). Cache bitset dominated masks and HV results, use a tiny focused swap pool, vectorize nondomination, and adapt sample/pool sizes to N, D, k for speed and reliable marginal estimates.
good_reflections: Prefilter non‑dominated candidates and remove points dominated by the reference. Use lazy greedy with a priority queue storing marginal HV contributions and only recompute when a candidate reaches the top. Cache incremental HV or use per‑point contribution routines instead of full recompute. Combine a cheap surrogate (R2) to shortlist candidates, then refine top ones with exact HV/local swaps. Vectorize mask math and minimize Python loops.

Always prefilter (non‑dominated), compute cheap single‑point estimates, drive a lazy max‑heap of marginal gains with stamps, recompute exact marginal HV only on heap top, then apply a small bounded local swap. Cache results, avoid rebuilding arrays/objects in loops, vectorize dominated checks, and cap stochastic search effort. This combo maximizes HV per evaluation at low runtime.

Prefer Pareto prefilter + stamped lazy greedy with single‑rect warmstarts and an adaptive shortlist (c·k). Cache HVs keyed cheaply (sorted tuple or bitmask), compute marginal = hv(S∪{p})−hv(S) only for heap tops, use pygmo when available, fallback to adaptive Monte‑Carlo with bitset chunking, and limit swap trials. Vectorize nondomination and avoid repeated frozenset/array conversions.

Keep the stamped lazy-greedy backbone: Pareto prefilter → adaptive rect‑surrogate shortlist → stamped heap + HV-cache → tiny bounded swap. Map original indices into a compact pool, compute HVs with pygmo when available, otherwise reuse a small shared MC sample for marginal estimates. Limit pairwise/diversity costs and cap shortlist/swap budgets adaptively by k to maximize HV per compute.

Seed a stamped lazy-greedy heap with normalized singleton rect scores, keep a compact shortlist ∝ k, cache HVs using canonical sorted tuples or bitmasks, and recompute exact marginal only for popped items. Reuse Monte‑Carlo dominated masks (bitsets) to compute marginals incrementally; adapt sample size to D and pool size. Limit and prioritize swap trials. Profile hotspots and avoid O(N^2) Python loops—vectorize or chunk.
bad_reflections: Favor a lazy‑greedy core: Pareto prefilter → top‑c·k shortlist by rect volumes → stamped heap with cached marginals and only top‑of‑heap exact HV recompute → bounded local swaps. Reuse arrays, vectorize dominance checks, cap Monte‑Carlo work, and call a C HV library (pygmo) or incremental HV routines to cut Python overhead.

Prefer Pareto prefilter + rect surrogate shortlist + stamped lazy heap, but enforce: (1) cached HVs keyed by frozenset(indices), (2) reuse a single pygmo.hypervolume object per distinct point set when possible, (3) vectorize dominance and sample precomputation, (4) adapt shortlist size to k and N, and (5) bound local swaps — this yields high HV with predictable runtime.

Always Pareto‑filter first, then shortlist by single‑point rect volumes plus a lightweight diversity pick. Use a stamped lazy greedy heap with exact HV from a fast C library; cache marginal gains keyed by compact bitmasks. Limit Monte‑Carlo to large‑D fallback with small M. Cap swap neighborhood and parallelize exact evaluations. Vectorize dominance checks and avoid hot Python loops or frequent frozenset creation.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: Pareto prefilter, stamped lazy greedy, rect surrogate, blended surrogate, bitset masks, HV cache, pygmo/MC fallback, adaptive shortlist, bounded swaps, vectorization.
- Advice: Prefilter non‑dominated points; shortlist ≈c·k via blended surrogate (rect + normalized shared MC + lightweight diversity); seed stamped lazy heap with surrogate scores; recompute exact marginal HV only for heap top; cache bitset dominated masks and HVs; use a tiny bounded swap pool; adapt MC/pool sizes to N, D, k; prefer pygmo when available.
- Avoid: Full HV recomputes for every candidate, O(N^2) Python loops, rebuilding arrays/frozensets in hot paths, unbounded swap/search budgets.
- Explanation: Concentrates costly exact evaluations on promising candidates, yields reliable marginals with adaptive sampling, and minimizes Python overhead through vectorization and caching.
